concepts.title: Large Numbers and Place Value
concepts.definition: Numbers can be extended to lakhs, crores and beyond, with each digit having a place value based on its position in the Indian or International system.
examples: In 7,36,52,081 the digit 7 is in the crore place, 3 in the ten‑lakh place, 6 in the lakh place, etc.
In the International system, 7,365,208 is read as seven million three hundred sixty‑five thousand two hundred eight.

concepts.title: Indian and International Numeration Systems
concepts.definition: Indian system groups digits as ones, tens, hundreds, thousands, lakhs and crores, while International system uses ones, thousands, millions, billions and so on.
examples: 75,23,608 is read in Indian system as seventy‑five lakh twenty‑three thousand six hundred eight.
The same number 7,523,608 is read in International system as seven million five hundred twenty‑three thousand six hundred eight.

concepts.title: Comparing and Ordering Numbers
concepts.definition: To compare large numbers, first compare number of digits; if equal, compare digit by digit from left to right.
examples: 9,85,432 > 7,23,999 because 9 is greater than 7 in the highest place.
Arranging 4521, 512, 9870 in ascending order gives 512, 4521, 9870.

concepts.title: Estimation and Rounding Off
concepts.definition: Estimation is approximating numbers to nearest tens, hundreds or thousands to make mental calculations easier.
examples: Rounding 348 to nearest ten gives 350; to nearest hundred gives 300.

concepts.title: Using Brackets and BODMAS
concepts.definition: Brackets show which operations to perform first in a calculation. BODMAS gives order: Brackets, Of, Division, Multiplication, Addition, Subtraction.
examples: In 12 ÷ 3 × 2, division and multiplication are done from left to right: (12 ÷ 3) × 2 = 4 × 2 = 8.

concepts.title: Roman Numerals (Intro)
concepts.definition: Roman numerals are a number system using letters like I, V, X, L, C, D and M for certain fixed values.

concepts.title: Natural and Whole Numbers
concepts.definition: Natural numbers are counting numbers 1, 2, 3, ...; whole numbers are natural numbers together with 0, i.e., 0, 1, 2, 3, ...
examples: 0 is a whole number but not a natural number.
5 is both a natural and a whole number.

concepts.title: Number Line Representation
concepts.definition: Whole numbers are represented on a line with equal spacing starting from 0 to the right indefinitely.
examples: Adding 2 on the number line means moving 2 steps to the right.
Subtracting 3 means moving 3 steps to the left.

concepts.title: Properties of Whole Numbers
concepts.definition: Whole numbers satisfy closure, commutative, associative and distributive properties for operations, and have an identity for addition and multiplication.
formulae: Closure (addition): a + b is a whole number for whole numbers a, b
Commutative: a + b = b + a; a × b = b × a
Associative: (a + b) + c = a + (b + c); (a × b) × c = a × (b × c)
Distributive: a × (b + c) = a × b + a × c
examples: 3 + 5 = 5 + 3 = 8 (commutative).

concepts.title: Patterns in Whole Numbers
concepts.definition: Whole numbers show patterns when arranged in grids or sequences, helping to discover properties about sums, differences and multiples.
examples: Even numbers occur at every second place on the number line.
Squares of numbers grow quickly: 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, ...

concepts.title: Factors and Multiples
concepts.definition: A factor divides a number exactly without remainder; a multiple is obtained by multiplying the number by integers.
examples: Factors of 12 are 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12.
Multiples of 5 are 5, 10, 15, 20, ...

concepts.title: Prime and Composite Numbers
concepts.definition: Prime numbers have exactly two distinct factors, 1 and the number itself; composite numbers have more than two factors.
examples: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11 are primes; 4, 6, 8, 9, 10 are composite.
1 is neither prime nor composite.

concepts.title: Divisibility Rules
concepts.definition: Divisibility tests use digit patterns to tell quickly whether a number is divisible by another without full division.
examples: A number divisible by 2 ends in 0, 2, 4, 6, 8.
Divisible by 3 if sum of digits is divisible by 3; by 5 if last digit is 0 or 5.

concepts.title: Common Factors and Common Multiples
concepts.definition: Common factors divide each of the given numbers; common multiples are multiples common to them.
examples: Common factors of 12 and 18 are 1, 2, 3, 6.
Common multiples of 3 and 4 include 12, 24, 36, ...

concepts.title: Prime Factorisation and HCF/LCM (Concept)
concepts.definition: A number can be written as product of prime factors. HCF is the highest common factor; LCM is the least common multiple of given numbers.
examples: Prime factorisation: 60 = 2² × 3 × 5.

concepts.title: Point, Line, Line Segment and Ray
concepts.definition: A point indicates a position; a line extends endlessly in both directions; a line segment has two endpoints; a ray has one endpoint and extends infinitely in one direction.
examples: Tip of a pencil is a point; edge of a ruler can represent a line segment.
Sun rays are like rays in geometry.

concepts.title: Curves and Polygons
concepts.definition: A curve is a drawing that may be straight or curved; a simple closed curve with straight sides is called a polygon.
examples: Triangle, square and pentagon are polygons.
A circle is a simple closed curve but not a polygon.

concepts.title: Angles and Their Naming
concepts.definition: An angle is formed when two rays start from a common endpoint. It is named using three letters with the vertex in the middle.
examples: ∠ABC has B as the vertex and BA, BC as arms.
Corners of a book show right angles.

concepts.title: Triangles and Quadrilaterals (Basic Idea)
concepts.definition: A triangle is a polygon with three sides and three angles; a quadrilateral has four sides and four angles.
examples: Roof gables are triangular; windows and doors are usually quadrilaterals.

concepts.title: Circles: Centre, Radius and Diameter
concepts.definition: A circle is the set of points at a fixed distance (radius) from a fixed point (centre). A diameter is a chord passing through the centre and is twice the radius.
examples: If radius is 3 cm, diameter is 6 cm.
If diameter is 10 cm, radius is 5 cm.

concepts.title: Measuring Line Segments
concepts.definition: Lengths of line segments are measured using a ruler or divider, ensuring the zero mark is aligned correctly and reading eye is vertical.
examples: A pencil may measure 9.2 cm on a ruler.

concepts.title: Types of Angles
concepts.definition: Angles are classified by measure as acute (0°–90°), right (90°), obtuse (90°–180°), straight (180°), reflex (180°–360°) and complete (360°).
examples: Angle of 45° is acute; 120° is obtuse; 180° is straight.

concepts.title: Triangles by Sides and Angles (Intro)
concepts.definition: Triangles can be classified as scalene (no equal sides), isosceles (two equal sides) or equilateral (all equal sides); by angles as acute, right or obtuse triangles.
examples: A triangle with sides 3 cm, 4 cm, 5 cm is scalene.
A triangle with all angles less than 90° is an acute triangle.

concepts.title: Perpendicular Lines and Perpendicular Bisectors
concepts.definition: Two lines are perpendicular if they meet to form a right angle; a perpendicular bisector cuts a line segment into two equal parts at 90°.
examples: Edges of a notebook form perpendicular lines.
Using a compass and ruler to construct the perpendicular bisector of a segment.

concepts.title: Integers and Number Line
concepts.definition: Integers include all positive and negative whole numbers and zero: ..., −3, −2, −1, 0, 1, 2, 3, ....
examples: -5, 0 and 7 are integers; 2.5 is not an integer.

concepts.title: Ordering Integers on Number Line
concepts.definition: On a number line, integers increase to the right and decrease to the left; any integer on the right is greater than one on its left.
examples: -3 < 0 < 4 because −3 is left of 0 and 4 is right of 0.

concepts.title: Addition of Integers on Number Line
concepts.definition: Addition is done by starting at the first integer on the number line and moving right for adding a positive integer and left for adding a negative integer.
examples: 2 + (−3) = −1; start at 2 and move 3 steps left.
−4 + 5 = 1; start at −4 and move 5 steps right.

concepts.title: Subtraction of Integers
concepts.definition: Subtracting an integer is equivalent to adding its additive inverse (the same number with opposite sign).

concepts.title: Definition and Types of Fractions
concepts.definition: A fraction represents a part of a whole and is written as numerator over denominator. Proper fractions have numerator less than denominator; improper fractions have numerator greater than or equal to denominator; mixed fractions combine a whole number and a proper fraction.
examples: 3/4 is proper; 9/4 is improper; 2 1/3 is mixed.

concepts.title: Fractions on Number Line
concepts.definition: Fractions are represented on a number line between integers by dividing the interval into equal parts equal to the denominator.
examples: 1/2 lies halfway between 0 and 1; 3/4 lies three‑quarters between 0 and 1.

concepts.title: Equivalent Fractions and Simplest Form
concepts.definition: Equivalent fractions have same value though numerators and denominators differ. Simplest form has numerator and denominator with no common factor other than 1.
examples: 1/2, 2/4 and 3/6 are equivalent fractions.
6/8 simplifies to 3/4.

concepts.title: Comparing and Ordering Fractions
concepts.definition: Fractions are compared using common denominators, cross‑multiplication or by visualising parts of the whole.
examples: To compare 2/3 and 3/5, compare 2 × 5 = 10 and 3 × 3 = 9; so 2/3 > 3/5.

concepts.title: Addition and Subtraction of Fractions
concepts.definition: Fractions with same denominators are combined by adding or subtracting numerators; for different denominators, a common denominator is used.

concepts.title: Place Value in Decimals
concepts.definition: Decimal numbers have whole number part and fractional part separated by a decimal point. Digits to the right have place values tenths, hundredths, thousandths, etc.
examples: In 4.37, 3 is tenths and 7 is hundredths.

concepts.title: Reading and Writing Decimals
concepts.definition: Decimals are read using place names after the point and can be written in expanded form showing each digit’s value.
examples: 3.45 is read as three point four five; 3.45 = 3 + 4/10 + 5/100.

concepts.title: Comparing and Ordering Decimals
concepts.definition: Decimals are compared by aligning decimal points and comparing digits from left to right, adding trailing zeros if needed.
examples: 0.7 = 0.70; 0.7 > 0.65 because 70 hundredths > 65 hundredths.

concepts.title: Addition and Subtraction of Decimals
concepts.definition: Decimals are added or subtracted by writing numbers one below the other with decimal points aligned, then operating like whole numbers.

concepts.title: Decimals in Measurement and Money
concepts.definition: Decimals are used to express money (rupees and paise) and measurements like length and weight with more precision.
examples: ₹5.75 means 5 rupees 75 paise; 1.25 m means 1 m 25 cm.

concepts.title: Data and Recording
concepts.definition: Data are collected information in the form of numbers or facts; they can be recorded in tables for clarity.
examples: Recording number of students absent on each day of a week.

concepts.title: Pictographs
concepts.definition: Pictographs use pictures or symbols to represent a certain number of items, giving a visual representation of data.
examples: One picture of a ball representing 5 balls in a pictograph of sports equipment.

concepts.title: Bar Graphs (Intro)
concepts.definition: Bar graphs display data using rectangular bars with lengths proportional to values, along horizontal and vertical axes.
examples: Bar graph showing number of books read by students in a month.

concepts.title: Perimeter of Simple Shapes
concepts.definition: Perimeter is the total length of the boundary of a closed figure.
examples: A rectangle of 5 cm by 3 cm has perimeter 2 × (5 + 3) = 16 cm.

concepts.title: Area of Rectangle and Square (Intro)
concepts.definition: Area is the region enclosed by a figure; for rectangles and squares it equals the product of side lengths.
examples: A square of side 4 cm has area 16 cm².

concepts.title: Applications of Perimeter and Area
concepts.definition: Perimeter and area are used to determine fencing length and surface required for tiling, carpeting, painting, etc.
examples: Finding wire needed to fence a rectangular park and paint needed for its floor.

concepts.title: Introduction to Variables and Constants
concepts.definition: A variable is a symbol (such as x, y) that can take different values; a constant is a fixed number.
examples: In 3x + 5, x is a variable and 3, 5 are constants (3 also acts as coefficient).

concepts.title: Simple Algebraic Expressions
concepts.definition: Algebraic expressions are formed by combining variables and constants using operations of addition, subtraction, multiplication and division.
examples: 2x + 3, 5y − 7, 3a + 2b + 4 are algebraic expressions.

concepts.title: Using Variables in Formulas and Rules
concepts.definition: Variables can represent general rules and formulas that work for many numbers.
examples: Perimeter of a rectangle with length l and breadth b is 2(l + b).
Cost of x pencils at ₹5 each is 5x.

concepts.title: Simple Equations (Intro)
concepts.definition: A simple equation states that two expressions are equal; solving means finding the value of the variable that satisfies the equation.
examples: x + 3 = 7 has solution x = 4.
2x = 12 has solution x = 6.

concepts.title: Ratio
concepts.definition: Ratio compares two quantities of the same kind by division, written as a : b.
examples: If there are 2 boys and 3 girls, the ratio boys : girls is 2 : 3.

concepts.title: Equivalent Ratios
concepts.definition: Ratios that represent the same comparison are equivalent; they can be obtained by multiplying or dividing both terms by the same number.
examples: 2 : 3 is equivalent to 4 : 6 or 6 : 9.

concepts.title: Proportion
concepts.definition: When two ratios are equal, they are said to be in proportion.
formulae: a : b = c : d ⇔ a/b = c/d
examples: If 2 : 3 = 4 : 6, then 2, 3, 4, 6 are in proportion.

concepts.title: Unitary Method (Intro)
concepts.definition: In the unitary method, first the value of a single unit is found, then used to find the value for required number of units.
examples: If 5 notebooks cost ₹40, cost of 1 notebook is ₹8; cost of 7 notebooks is ₹56.

concepts.title: Line of Symmetry
concepts.definition: A figure has line symmetry if it can be folded along a line so that both halves match exactly.
examples: A square has 4 lines of symmetry; an equilateral triangle has 3.

concepts.title: Symmetry in Everyday Objects
concepts.definition: Many natural and man‑made objects show symmetry, making them appear balanced and attractive.
examples: Butterflies, leaves and many logos have line symmetry.

concepts.title: Instruments of Geometry Box
concepts.definition: A geometry box contains ruler, compass, divider, protractor and set‑squares used for constructions.
examples: Ruler measures lengths; compass draws circles; protractor measures angles.

concepts.title: Drawing Line Segments
concepts.definition: A line segment of given length is drawn using a ruler and marking endpoints at required distance.
examples: To draw segment of length 5.6 cm, mark 0 and 5.6 cm on a ruler and join them.

concepts.title: Constructing Circles
concepts.definition: A circle is drawn by fixing the compass point at the centre and pencil at a distance equal to the radius, then rotating once around.
examples: Constructing a circle of radius 3 cm using compass and ruler.

concepts.title: Perpendiculars and Perpendicular Bisectors
concepts.definition: Perpendicular lines make a 90° angle; perpendicular bisector of a segment passes through its midpoint at right angle.
examples: Using compass arcs from endpoints of a segment to locate perpendicular bisector.

concepts.title: Angles of Special Measures
concepts.definition: Angles of 30°, 45°, 60°, 90° etc. are constructed using compass and straightedge by standard geometric methods.
examples: Construct a 60° angle by drawing an equilateral triangle; then bisect to get 30°.
